title: practical english usage - Part 32
content:
2 progressive         forms     not   used I think we have mice.

She has a houseful of children this weekend.

Sometimes have simply expresses the fact of being in a particular situation.

My grandmother didn't have a very nice personality.

The Prime Minister had a bad cold.

Do you have any brothers or sisters?

They hardly have enough money to live on.

Her father has an office in Westminster.

characteristics of people and things, and similar ideas.

We   often use have to talk about states: possession, relationships, illnesses, the 1 meanings other     states 24 have      (got):  possession,       relationships         and Be,  have      and   do     Section 2 grammar •  25  be and have be right, be wrong be lucky Note also: be afraid not be hungry (     have hunger), be thirsty, be warm, be hot, be cold, be sleepy, Note the following expressions: or physical conditions, we  normally use be (   feel) + adjective, not have + noun.

To  talk about experiencing hunger, thirst, heat, cold and certain other common 1  physical      conditions:       hunger,     thirst,    etc 25 be   and   have Do you have time to go to the beach this weekend? (AmE / modern BrE) do-forms are common even when there is no idea of repetition.

ɪn modern British English (which is heavily influenced by American English), Have you (got) a meeting today?

Do you often have meetings?

habit or repetition. Compare: Traditionally in British English, do was used with have mostly in references to 8  repetition:      a  change      in  British    English We don't usually have beer in the house.

- Sorry, I don't have / haven’ t got any beer.

Do you ever have time to go to London?

- Do you have / Have you got time to go to London this weekend?

I often have headaches.

- I have / I’ ve got a headache.

often used. Compare: When we  are talking about repeated or habitual states, got-forms of have are less 7 repeated       states:    got    not   used 'I’ ve got a new apartment.’ 'You do?

answers, reply questions and tags follow got-forms.

Got- and do-forms may be mixed in American English, especially when short I(’ve) got a problem.

In very informal American speech, people may drop’ ve (but not 's) before got.

negatives.

Have got is rather less common in American English, especially in questions and boyfriend).

of have got is occasionally used after modal verbs (e.g. She must  have got a new cannot usually say to have got a headache or having got a brother. The  infinitive Got is not generally used with infinitives, participles or -ing forms of have: you Did you have good teachers when you were at school?

not                                                      L..) I had some problems last week, (     I-had-got-some-pi Got-forms of have are less common in the past tense.

Anne’s got a bike, hasn't she?

'Have you got a light? 'No, I haven't.’ (     N , I haven’t got.) Got-forms of have are not used in short answers or tags.

grammar •  26  do: introduction The company didn't do very well last year.

Do you do much  gardening? How do you do?

Auxiliary do and non-auxiliary do can occur together.

4 combined        forms He told me to open the door. I did it as quietly as I could.

I need to take a rest, and I shall do so as soon as I can find time.

repeating another verb and what follows. For details, ►    29.

Do so/it/that can be used as a substitute expression when we  want to avoid 'Do you think Jake will come? 'He might do.' (AmE He might.) auxiliary. For details, ►    28.

In British English, do can be used alone as a substitute for a main verb after an 3 substitute        verb I've finished the phone calls, and I'll do the letters tomorrow.

What are you doing? Don't just stand there. Do something.

For details, and the difference between do and make, ►    435.

activity, and is used when it is not necessary or not possible to be more precise.

Do is also an ordinary (non-auxiliary) verb. It can refer to almost any kind of 2 general-purpose           verb I do like your earrings. 'Jack eats too much.' 'He certainly does.' Did you remember to buy milk? This doesn't taste very nice.
